    89→// extractMediaType returns the mediaType of a required parser. It tries first to
    90→// extract a valid and supported mediaType from contentType. If that fails,
    91→// the provided fallbackType (possibly an empty string) is returned, together with
    92→// an error. fallbackType is used as-is without further validation.
    93→func extractMediaType(contentType, fallbackType string) (string, error) {
    94→	if contentType == "" {
    95→		if fallbackType == "" {
    96→			return "", errors.New("non-compliant scrape target sending blank Content-Type and no fallback_scrape_protocol specified for target")
    97→		}
    98→		return fallbackType, fmt.Errorf("non-compliant scrape target sending blank Content-Type, using fallback_scrape_protocol %q", fallbackType)
    99→	}
   100→
   101→	// We have a contentType, parse it.
   102→	mediaType, _, err := mime.ParseMediaType(contentType)
   103→	if err != nil {
   104→		if fallbackType == "" {
   105→			retErr := fmt.Errorf("cannot parse Content-Type %q and no fallback_scrape_protocol for target", contentType)
   106→			return "", errors.Join(retErr, err)
   107→		}
   108→		retErr := fmt.Errorf("could not parse received Content-Type %q, using fallback_scrape_protocol %q", contentType, fallbackType)
   109→		return fallbackType, errors.Join(retErr, err)
   110→	}
   111→
   112→	// We have a valid media type, either we recognise it and can use it
   113→	// or we have to error.
   114→	switch mediaType {
   115→	case "application/openmetrics-text", "application/vnd.google.protobuf", "text/plain":
   116→		return mediaType, nil
   117→	}
   118→	// We're here because we have no recognised mediaType.
   119→	if fallbackType == "" {
   120→		return "", fmt.Errorf("received unsupported Content-Type %q and no fallback_scrape_protocol specified for target", contentType)
   121→	}
   122→	return fallbackType, fmt.Errorf("received unsupported Content-Type %q, using fallback_scrape_protocol %q", contentType, fallbackType)
   123→}
   124→
   125→type ParserOptions struct {
   126→	// EnableTypeAndUnitLabels enables parsing and inclusion of type and unit labels
   127→	// in the parsed metrics.
   128→	EnableTypeAndUnitLabels bool
   129→
   130→	// IgnoreNativeHistograms causes the parser to completely ignore all
   131→	// parts of native histograms, but to keep the ability to convert
   132→	// classic histograms to NHCB. This has the implication that even a
   133→	// histogram that has some native parts but not a single classic bucket
   134→	// will be parsed as a classic histogram (with only the +Inf bucket and
   135→	// count and sum). Setting this also allows converting a classic
   136→	// histogram that already has a native representation to an NHCB. This
   137→	// option has no effect on parsers for formats that do not support
   138→	// native histograms.
   139→	IgnoreNativeHistograms bool
   140→
   141→	// ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB enables conversion of classic histograms
   142→	// to native histogram custom buckets (NHCB) format.
   143→	ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB bool
   144→
   145→	// KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms causes parser to output classic histogram
   146→	// that is also present as a native histogram. (Proto parsing only).
   147→	KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms bool
   148→
   149→	// OpenMetricsSkipSTSeries determines whether to skip `_created` timestamp series
   150→	// during (OpenMetrics parsing only).
   151→	OpenMetricsSkipSTSeries bool
   152→
   153→	// FallbackContentType specifies the fallback content type to use when the provided
   154→	// Content-Type header cannot be parsed or is not supported.
   155→	FallbackContentType string
   156→}
   157→
   158→// New returns a new parser of the byte slice.
   159→//
   160→// This function no longer guarantees to return a valid parser.
   161→//
   162→// It only returns a valid parser if the supplied contentType and fallbackType allow.
   163→// An error may also be returned if fallbackType had to be used or there was some
   164→// other error parsing the supplied Content-Type.
   165→// If the returned parser is nil then the scrape must fail.
   166→func New(b []byte, contentType string, st *labels.SymbolTable, opts ParserOptions) (Parser, error) {
   167→	if st == nil {
   168→		st = labels.NewSymbolTable()
   169→	}
   170→
   171→	mediaType, err := extractMediaType(contentType, opts.FallbackContentType)
   172→	// err may be nil or something we want to warn about.
   173→
   174→	var baseParser Parser
   175→	switch mediaType {
   176→	case "application/openmetrics-text":
   177→		baseParser = NewOpenMetricsParser(b, st, func(o *openMetricsParserOptions) {
   178→			o.skipSTSeries = opts.OpenMetricsSkipSTSeries
   179→			o.enableTypeAndUnitLabels = opts.EnableTypeAndUnitLabels
   180→		})
   181→	case "application/vnd.google.protobuf":
   182→		return NewProtobufParser(
   183→			b,
   184→			opts.IgnoreNativeHistograms,
   185→			opts.KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms,
   186→			opts.ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB,
   187→			opts.EnableTypeAndUnitLabels,
   188→			st,
   189→		), err
   190→	case "text/plain":
   191→		baseParser = NewPromParser(b, st, opts.EnableTypeAndUnitLabels)
   192→	default:
   193→		return nil, err
   194→	}
   195→
   196→	if baseParser != nil && opts.ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB {
   197→		baseParser = NewNHCBParser(baseParser, st, opts.KeepClassicOnClassicAndNativeHistograms)
   198→	}
   199→
   200→	return baseParser, err
   201→}
   202→
   203→// Entry represents the type of a parsed entry.
   204→type Entry int
   205→
   206→const (
   207→	EntryInvalid   Entry = -1
   208→	EntryType      Entry = 0
   209→	EntryHelp      Entry = 1
   210→	EntrySeries    Entry = 2 // EntrySeries marks a series with a simple float64 as value.
   211→	EntryComment   Entry = 3
   212→	EntryUnit      Entry = 4
   213→	EntryHistogram Entry = 5 // EntryHistogram marks a series with a native histogram as a value.
   214→)

    24→// CounterResetHint contains the known information about a counter reset,
    25→// or alternatively that we are dealing with a gauge histogram, where counter resets do not apply.
    26→type CounterResetHint byte
    27→
    28→const (
    29→	UnknownCounterReset CounterResetHint = iota // UnknownCounterReset means we cannot say if this histogram signals a counter reset or not.
    30→	CounterReset                                // CounterReset means there was definitely a counter reset starting from this histogram.
    31→	NotCounterReset                             // NotCounterReset means there was definitely no counter reset with this histogram.
    32→	GaugeType                                   // GaugeType means this is a gauge histogram, where counter resets do not happen.
    33→)
    34→
    35→// Histogram encodes a sparse, high-resolution histogram. See the design
    36→// document for full details:
    37→// https://docs.google.com/document/d/1cLNv3aufPZb3fNfaJgdaRBZsInZKKIHo9E6HinJVbpM/edit#
    38→//
    39→// The most tricky bit is how bucket indices represent real bucket boundaries.
    40→// An example for schema 0 (by which each bucket is twice as wide as the
    41→// previous bucket):
    42→//
    43→//	Bucket boundaries →              [-2,-1)  [-1,-0.5) [-0.5,-0.25) ... [-0.001,0.001] ... (0.25,0.5] (0.5,1]  (1,2] ....
    44→//	                                    ↑        ↑           ↑                  ↑                ↑         ↑      ↑
    45→//	Zero bucket (width e.g. 0.001) →    |        |           |                  ZB               |         |      |
    46→//	Positive bucket indices →           |        |           |                          ...     -1         0      1    2    3
    47→//	Negative bucket indices →  3   2    1        0          -1       ...
    48→//
    49→// Which bucket indices are actually used is determined by the spans.
    50→type Histogram struct {
    51→	// Counter reset information.
    52→	CounterResetHint CounterResetHint
    53→	// Currently valid schema numbers are -4 <= n <= 8 for exponential buckets,
    54→	// They are all for base-2 bucket schemas, where 1 is a bucket boundary in
    55→	// each case, and then each power of two is divided into 2^n logarithmic buckets.
    56→	// Or in other words, each bucket boundary is the previous boundary times
    57→	// 2^(2^-n). Another valid schema number is -53 for custom buckets, defined by
    58→	// the CustomValues field.
    59→	Schema int32
    60→	// Width of the zero bucket.
    61→	ZeroThreshold float64
    62→	// Observations falling into the zero bucket.
    63→	ZeroCount uint64
    64→	// Total number of observations.
    65→	Count uint64
    66→	// Sum of observations. This is also used as the stale marker.
    67→	Sum float64
    68→	// Spans for positive and negative buckets (see Span below).
    69→	PositiveSpans, NegativeSpans []Span
    70→	// Observation counts in buckets. The first element is an absolute
    71→	// count. All following ones are deltas relative to the previous
    72→	// element.
    73→	PositiveBuckets, NegativeBuckets []int64
    74→	// Holds the custom (usually upper) bounds for bucket definitions, otherwise nil.
    75→	// This slice is interned, to be treated as immutable and copied by reference.
    76→	// These numbers should be strictly increasing. This field is only used when the
    77→	// schema is for custom buckets, and the ZeroThreshold, ZeroCount, NegativeSpans
    78→	// and NegativeBuckets fields are not used in that case.
    79→	CustomValues []float64
    80→}
    81→
    82→// A Span defines a continuous sequence of buckets.
    83→type Span struct {
    84→	// Gap to previous span (always positive), or starting index for the 1st
    85→	// span (which can be negative).
    86→	Offset int32
    87→	// Length of the span.
    88→	Length uint32
    89→}

    91→func (h *Histogram) UsesCustomBuckets() bool {
    92→	return IsCustomBucketsSchema(h.Schema)
    93→}

    43→// Label is a key/value a pair of strings.

    48→func (ls Labels) String() string {
    49→	return ls.stringImpl(true)
    50→}
    51→
    52→// StringNoSpace is like String but does not add a space after commas.
    53→func (ls Labels) StringNoSpace() string {

    57→func (ls Labels) stringImpl(addSpace bool) string {
    58→	var bytea [1024]byte // On stack to avoid memory allocation while building the output.
    59→	b := bytes.NewBuffer(bytea[:0])
    60→
    61→	b.WriteByte('{')
    62→	i := 0
    63→	ls.Range(func(l Label) {
    64→		if i > 0 {
    65→			b.WriteByte(',')
    66→			if addSpace {
    67→				b.WriteByte(' ')
    68→			}
    69→		}
    70→		if !model.LegacyValidation.IsValidLabelName(l.Name) {
    71→			b.Write(strconv.AppendQuote(b.AvailableBuffer(), l.Name))
    72→		} else {
    73→			b.WriteString(l.Name)
    74→		}
    75→		b.WriteByte('=')
    76→		b.Write(strconv.AppendQuote(b.AvailableBuffer(), l.Value))
    77→		i++
    78→	})
    79→	b.WriteByte('}')
    80→	return b.String()
    81→}

    38→	ErrTooOldSample = errors.New("too old sample")
    39→	// ErrDuplicateSampleForTimestamp is when the sample has same timestamp but different value.
    40→	ErrDuplicateSampleForTimestamp = errDuplicateSampleForTimestamp{}
    41→	ErrOutOfOrderExemplar          = errors.New("out of order exemplar")
    42→	ErrDuplicateExemplar           = errors.New("duplicate exemplar")
    43→	ErrExemplarLabelLength         = fmt.Errorf("label length for exemplar exceeds maximum of %d UTF-8 characters", exemplar.ExemplarMaxLabelSetLength)
    44→	ErrExemplarsDisabled           = errors.New("exemplar storage is disabled or max exemplars is less than or equal to 0")
    45→	ErrNativeHistogramsDisabled    = errors.New("native histograms are disabled")
    46→
    47→	// ErrOutOfOrderST indicates failed append of ST to the storage
    48→	// due to ST being older the then newer sample.
    49→	// NOTE(bwplotka): This can be both an instrumentation failure or commonly expected
    50→	// behaviour, and we currently don't have a way to determine this. As a result
    51→	// it's recommended to ignore this error for now.
    52→	// TODO(bwplotka): Remove with appender v1 flow; not used in v2.
    53→	ErrOutOfOrderST      = errors.New("start timestamp out of order, ignoring")
    54→	ErrSTNewerThanSample = errors.New("ST is newer or the same as sample's timestamp, ignoring")
    55→)

   203→// SelectHints specifies hints passed for data selections.
   204→// This is used only as an option for implementation to use.
   205→type SelectHints struct {
   206→	Start int64 // Start time in milliseconds for this select.
   207→	End   int64 // End time in milliseconds for this select.
   208→
   209→	// Maximum number of results returned. Use a value of 0 to disable.
   210→	Limit int
   211→
   212→	Step int64  // Query step size in milliseconds.
   213→	Func string // String representation of surrounding function or aggregation.
   214→
   215→	Grouping []string // List of label names used in aggregation.
   216→	By       bool     // Indicate whether it is without or by.
   217→	Range    int64    // Range vector selector range in milliseconds.
   218→
   219→	// ShardCount is the total number of shards that series should be split into
   220→	// at query time. Then, only series in the ShardIndex shard will be returned
   221→	// by the query.
   222→	//
   223→	// ShardCount equal to 0 means that sharding is disabled.
   224→	ShardCount uint64
   225→
   226→	// ShardIndex is the series shard index to query. The index must be between 0 and ShardCount-1.
   227→	// When ShardCount is set to a value > 0, then a query will only process series within the
   228→	// ShardIndex's shard.
   229→	//
   230→	// Series are sharded by "labels stable hash" mod "ShardCount".
   231→	ShardIndex uint64
   232→
   233→	// DisableTrimming allows to disable trimming of matching series chunks based on query Start and End time.
   234→	// When disabled, the result may contain samples outside the queried time range but Select() performances
   235→	// may be improved.
   236→	DisableTrimming bool
   237→
   238→	// Projection hints. They are currently unused in the Prometheus promql engine but can be used by different
   239→	// implementations of the Queryable interface and engines.
   240→	// These hints are useful for queries like `sum by (label) (rate(metric[5m]))` - we can safely evaluate it
   241→	// even if we only fetch the `label` label. For some storage implementations this is beneficial.
   242→
   243→	// ProjectionLabels are the minimum amount of labels required to be fetched for this Select call
   244→	// When honored it is required to add an __series_hash__ label containing the hash of all labels
   245→	// of a particular series so that the engine can still perform horizontal joins.
   246→	ProjectionLabels []string
   247→
   248→	// ProjectionInclude defines if we have to include or exclude the labels from the ProjectLabels field.
   249→	ProjectionInclude bool
   250→}

   271→// WARNING(bwplotka): Switch to AppendableV2 is in progress (https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/17632).

   280→// Appender provides batched appends against a storage.
   281→// It must be completed with a call to Commit or Rollback and must not be reused afterwards.
   282→//
   283→// Operations on the Appender interface are not goroutine-safe.
   284→//
   285→// The order of samples appended via the Appender is preserved within each series.
   286→// I.e. timestamp order within batch is not validated, samples are not reordered per timestamp or by float/histogram
   287→// type.
   288→//
   289→// WARNING(bwplotka): Switch to AppendableV2 is in progress (https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/17632).
   290→// Appender will be removed soon (ETA: Q2 2026).
   291→type Appender interface {
   292→	AppenderTransaction
   293→
   294→	// Append adds a sample pair for the given series.
   295→	// An optional series reference can be provided to accelerate calls.
   296→	// A series reference number is returned which can be used to add further
   297→	// samples to the given series in the same or later transactions.
   298→	// Returned reference numbers are ephemeral and may be rejected in calls
   299→	// to Append() at any point. Adding the sample via Append() returns a new
   300→	// reference number.
   301→	// If the reference is 0 it must not be used for caching.
   302→	Append(ref SeriesRef, l labels.Labels, t int64, v float64) (SeriesRef, error)
   303→
   304→	// SetOptions configures the appender with specific append options such as
   305→	// discarding out-of-order samples even if out-of-order is enabled in the TSDB.
   306→	SetOptions(opts *AppendOptions)
   307→
   308→	ExemplarAppender
   309→	HistogramAppender
   310→	MetadataUpdater
   311→	StartTimestampAppender
   312→}
   313→
   314→// GetRef is an extra interface on Appenders used by downstream projects
   315→// (e.g. Cortex) to avoid maintaining a parallel set of references.
   316→type GetRef interface {
   317→	// GetRef returns a reference number that can be used to pass to AppenderV2.Append(),
   318→	// and a set of labels that will not cause another copy when passed to AppenderV2.Append().
   319→	// 0 means the appender does not have a reference to this series.
   320→	// hash should be a hash of lset.
   321→	GetRef(lset labels.Labels, hash uint64) (SeriesRef, labels.Labels)
   322→}

   324→// ExemplarAppender provides an interface for adding samples to exemplar storage, which
   325→// within Prometheus is in-memory only.

   327→// WARNING(bwplotka): Switch to AppendableV2 is in progress (https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/17632).
   328→// ExemplarAppender will be removed soon (ETA: Q2 2026).
   329→type ExemplarAppender interface {
   330→	// AppendExemplar adds an exemplar for the given series labels.

   332→	// A reference number is returned which can be used to add further
   333→	// exemplars in the same or later transactions.
   334→	// Returned reference numbers are ephemeral and may be rejected in calls
   335→	// to Append() at any point. Adding the sample via Append() returns a new
   336→	// reference number.
   337→	// If the reference is 0 it must not be used for caching.
   338→	// Note that in our current implementation of Prometheus' exemplar storage
   339→	// calls to Append should generate the reference numbers, AppendExemplar
   340→	// generating a new reference number should be considered possible erroneous behaviour and be logged.

   344→// HistogramAppender provides an interface for appending histograms to the storage.
   345→//
   346→// WARNING(bwplotka): Switch to AppendableV2 is in progress (https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/17632).
   347→// HistogramAppender will be removed soon (ETA: Q2 2026).
   348→type HistogramAppender interface {
   349→	// AppendHistogram adds a histogram for the given series labels. An
   350→	// optional reference number can be provided to accelerate calls. A
   351→	// reference number is returned which can be used to add further
   352→	// histograms in the same or later transactions. Returned reference
   353→	// numbers are ephemeral and may be rejected in calls to Append() at any
   354→	// point. Adding the sample via Append() returns a new reference number.
   355→	// If the reference is 0, it must not be used for caching.
   356→	//
   357→	// For efficiency reasons, the histogram is passed as a
   358→	// pointer. AppendHistogram won't mutate the histogram, but in turn
   359→	// depends on the caller to not mutate it either.
   360→	AppendHistogram(ref SeriesRef, l labels.Labels, t int64, h *histogram.Histogram, fh *histogram.FloatHistogram) (SeriesRef, error)
   361→	// AppendHistogramSTZeroSample adds synthetic zero sample for the given st timestamp,
   362→	// which will be associated with given series, labels and the incoming
   363→	// sample's t (timestamp). AppendHistogramSTZeroSample returns error if zero sample can't be
   364→	// appended, for example when st is too old, or when it would collide with
   365→	// incoming sample (sample has priority).
   366→	//
   367→	// AppendHistogramSTZeroSample has to be called before the corresponding histogram AppendHistogram.
   368→	// A series reference number is returned which can be used to modify the
   369→	// ST for the given series in the same or later transactions.
   370→	// Returned reference numbers are ephemeral and may be rejected in calls
   371→	// to AppendHistogramSTZeroSample() at any point.
   372→	//
   373→	// If the reference is 0 it must not be used for caching.
   374→	AppendHistogramSTZeroSample(ref SeriesRef, l labels.Labels, t, st int64, h *histogram.Histogram, fh *histogram.FloatHistogram) (SeriesRef, error)
   375→}

    40→// ExponentialBlockRanges returns the time ranges based on the stepSize.
    41→func ExponentialBlockRanges(minSize int64, steps, stepSize int) []int64 {
    42→	ranges := make([]int64, 0, steps)
    43→	curRange := minSize
    44→	for range steps {
    45→		ranges = append(ranges, curRange)
    46→		curRange *= int64(stepSize)
    47→	}
    48→
    49→	return ranges
    50→}

    52→// Compactor provides compaction against an underlying storage
    53→// of time series data.
    54→type Compactor interface {
    55→	// Plan returns a set of directories that can be compacted concurrently.
    56→	// The directories can be overlapping.
    57→	// Results returned when compactions are in progress are undefined.
    58→	Plan(dir string) ([]string, error)
    59→
    60→	// Write persists one or more Blocks into a directory.
    61→	// No Block is written when resulting Block has 0 samples and returns an empty slice.
    62→	// Prometheus always return one or no block. The interface allows returning more than one
    63→	// block for downstream users to experiment with compactor.
    64→	Write(dest string, b BlockReader, mint, maxt int64, base *BlockMeta) ([]ulid.ULID, error)
    65→
    66→	// Compact runs compaction against the provided directories. Must
    67→	// only be called concurrently with results of Plan().
    68→	// Can optionally pass a list of already open blocks,
    69→	// to avoid having to reopen them.
    70→	// Prometheus always return one or no block. The interface allows returning more than one
    71→	// block for downstream users to experiment with compactor.
    72→	// When one resulting Block has 0 samples
    73→	//  * No block is written.
    74→	//  * The source dirs are marked Deletable.
    75→	//  * Block is not included in the result.
    76→	Compact(dest string, dirs []string, open []*Block) ([]ulid.ULID, error)
    77→}

    53→	// KeepEqual drops targets for which the input does not match the target.
    54→	KeepEqual Action = "keepequal"
    55→	// DropEqual drops targets for which the input does match the target.

    62→	LabelDrop Action = "labeldrop"
    63→	// LabelKeep drops any label not matching the regex.
    64→	LabelKeep Action = "labelkeep"
    65→	// Lowercase maps input letters to their lower case.
    66→	Lowercase Action = "lowercase"
    67→	// Uppercase maps input letters to their upper case.

    73→	var s string
    74→	if err := unmarshal(&s); err != nil {
    75→		return err
    76→	}
    77→	switch act := Action(strings.ToLower(s)); act {
    78→	case Replace, Keep, Drop, HashMod, LabelMap, LabelDrop, LabelKeep, Lowercase, Uppercase, KeepEqual, DropEqual:
    79→		*a = act
    80→		return nil

    34→// metrics with identical labelsets after applying rule labels.

    36→
    37→// A RecordingRule records its vector expression into new timeseries.
    38→type RecordingRule struct {
    39→	name   string
    40→	vector parser.Expr
    41→	labels labels.Labels
    42→	// The health of the recording rule.
    43→	health *atomic.String
    44→	// Timestamp of last evaluation of the recording rule.
    45→	evaluationTimestamp *atomic.Time
    46→	// The last error seen by the recording rule.
    47→	lastError *atomic.Error
    48→	// Duration of how long it took to evaluate the recording rule.
    49→	evaluationDuration *atomic.Duration
    50→
    51→	dependenciesMutex sync.RWMutex
    52→	dependentRules    []Rule
    53→	dependencyRules   []Rule
    54→}
    55→
    56→// NewRecordingRule returns a new recording rule.
    57→func NewRecordingRule(name string, vector parser.Expr, lset labels.Labels) *RecordingRule {
    58→	return &RecordingRule{
    59→		name:                name,
    60→		vector:              vector,
    61→		labels:              lset,
    62→		health:              atomic.NewString(string(HealthUnknown)),
    63→		evaluationTimestamp: atomic.NewTime(time.Time{}),
    64→		evaluationDuration:  atomic.NewDuration(0),
    65→		lastError:           atomic.NewError(nil),
    66→	}
    67→}

    69→// Name returns the rule name.
    70→func (rule *RecordingRule) Name() string {
    71→	return rule.name
    72→}
    73→
    74→// Query returns the rule query expression.
    75→func (rule *RecordingRule) Query() parser.Expr {
    76→	return rule.vector
    77→}
    78→
    79→// Labels returns the rule labels.
    80→func (rule *RecordingRule) Labels() labels.Labels {
    81→	return rule.labels
    82→}
    83→
    84→// Eval evaluates the rule and then overrides the metric names and labels accordingly.
    85→func (rule *RecordingRule) Eval(ctx context.Context, queryOffset time.Duration, ts time.Time, query QueryFunc, _ *url.URL, limit int) (promql.Vector, error) {
    86→	ctx = NewOriginContext(ctx, NewRuleDetail(rule))
    87→	vector, err := query(ctx, rule.vector.String(), ts.Add(-queryOffset))
    88→	if err != nil {
    89→		return nil, err
    90→	}
    91→
    92→	// Override the metric name and labels.
    93→	lb := labels.NewBuilder(labels.EmptyLabels())
    94→
    95→	for i := range vector {
    96→		sample := &vector[i]
    97→
    98→		lb.Reset(sample.Metric)
    99→		lb.Set(labels.MetricName, rule.name)
   100→
   101→		rule.labels.Range(func(l labels.Label) {
   102→			lb.Set(l.Name, l.Value)
   103→		})
   104→
   105→		sample.Metric = lb.Labels()
   106→	}
   107→
   108→	// Check that the rule does not produce identical metrics after applying
   109→	// labels.
   110→	if vector.ContainsSameLabelset() {
   111→		return nil, ErrDuplicateRecordingLabelSet
   112→	}
   113→
   114→	numSeries := len(vector)
   115→	if limit > 0 && numSeries > limit {
   116→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("exceeded limit of %d with %d series", limit, numSeries)
   117→	}
   118→
   119→	rule.SetHealth(HealthGood)
   120→	rule.SetLastError(err)
   121→	return vector, nil
   122→}

   130→
   131→	byt, err := yaml.Marshal(r)
   132→	if err != nil {

   135→
   136→	return string(byt)
   137→}

   705→// acceptHeader transforms preference from the options into specific header values as
   706→// https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#name-accept defines.
   707→// No validation is here, we expect scrape protocols to be validated already.
   708→func acceptHeader(sps []config.ScrapeProtocol, scheme model.EscapingScheme) string {
   709→	var vals []string
   710→	weight := len(config.ScrapeProtocolsHeaders) + 1
   711→	for _, sp := range sps {
   712→		val := config.ScrapeProtocolsHeaders[sp]
   713→		// Escaping header is only valid for newer versions of the text formats.
   714→		if sp == config.PrometheusText1_0_0 || sp == config.OpenMetricsText1_0_0 {
   715→			val += ";" + model.EscapingKey + "=" + scheme.String()
   716→		}
   717→		val += fmt.Sprintf(";q=0.%d", weight)
   718→		vals = append(vals, val)
   719→		weight--
   720→	}
   721→	// Default match anything.
   722→	vals = append(vals, fmt.Sprintf("*/*;q=0.%d", weight))
   723→	return strings.Join(vals, ",")
   724→}

   735→func (s *targetScraper) scrape(ctx context.Context) (*http.Response, error) {
   736→	if s.req == nil {
   737→		req, err := http.NewRequest(http.MethodGet, s.URL().String(), nil)
   738→		if err != nil {
   739→			return nil, err
   740→		}
   741→		req.Header.Add("Accept", s.acceptHeader)
   742→		req.Header.Add("Accept-Encoding", s.acceptEncodingHeader)
   743→		req.Header.Set("User-Agent", UserAgent)
   744→		req.Header.Set("X-Prometheus-Scrape-Timeout-Seconds", strconv.FormatFloat(s.timeout.Seconds(), 'f', -1, 64))
   745→
   746→		s.req = req
   747→	}
   748→	ctx, span := otel.Tracer("").Start(ctx, "Scrape", trace.WithSpanKind(trace.SpanKindClient))
   749→	defer span.End()

   751→	return s.client.Do(s.req.WithContext(ctx))

   754→func (s *targetScraper) readResponse(_ context.Context, resp *http.Response, w io.Writer) (string, error) {
   755→	defer func() {
   756→		io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body)
   757→		resp.Body.Close()
   758→	}()
   759→
   760→	if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
   761→		return "", fmt.Errorf("server returned HTTP status %s", resp.Status)
   762→	}
   763→
   764→	if s.bodySizeLimit <= 0 {
   765→		s.bodySizeLimit = math.MaxInt64
   766→	}
   767→	if resp.Header.Get("Content-Encoding") != "gzip" {
   768→		n, err := io.Copy(w, io.LimitReader(resp.Body, s.bodySizeLimit))
   769→		if err != nil {
   770→			return "", err
   771→		}
   772→		if n >= s.bodySizeLimit {
   773→			s.metrics.targetScrapeExceededBodySizeLimit.Inc()
   774→			return "", errBodySizeLimit
   775→		}
   776→		return resp.Header.Get("Content-Type"), nil
   777→	}
   778→
   779→	if s.gzipr == nil {
   780→		s.buf = bufio.NewReader(resp.Body)

   782→		s.gzipr, err = gzip.NewReader(s.buf)
   783→		if err != nil {
   784→			return "", err
   785→		}
   786→	} else {
   787→		s.buf.Reset(resp.Body)
   788→		if err := s.gzipr.Reset(s.buf); err != nil {
   789→			return "", err

---

## Comprehensive Summary: Prometheus Metrics Scraping and Collection

All files are located at `/go/agent-index-go/testdata/fixtures/go/`. They are excerpts from real Prometheus source packages.

---

### 1. Scrape Manager (`manager.go`) - The Top-Level Coordinator

**Package:** `scrape`

**Type: `Manager`**
```go
type Manager struct {
    opts         *Options
    appendable   storage.Appendable    // v1 storage interface
    appendableV2 storage.AppendableV2  // v2 storage interface (preferred)
    scrapeConfigs map[string]*config.ScrapeConfig
    scrapePools   map[string]*scrapePool
    targetSets    map[string][]*targetgroup.Group
    graceShut     chan struct{}
    triggerReload chan struct{}
    metrics       *scrapeMetrics
    buffers       *pool.Pool
    mtxScrape     sync.Mutex
}
```

**Key functions:**
- `NewManager(...)` - validates that exactly one of `appendable`/`appendableV2` is supplied, creates `scrapeMetrics`, allocates a byte-pool (`pool.New(1e3, 100e6, 3, ...)`) for reusing read buffers
- `Run(tsets <-chan map[string][]*targetgroup.Group)` - main loop: receives target groups from discovery, debounces into `triggerReload` channel, delegates to `reloader()` goroutine
- `reloader()` - throttled via ticker (default 5s), drains `triggerReload` then calls `reload()`
- `reload()` - for each job that has no pool yet, creates a new `scrapePool`; calls `sp.Sync(groups)` in parallel goroutines
- `ApplyConfig(cfg)` - reloads each pool concurrently (bounded by `GOMAXPROCS`), stops removed pools, calls `sp.reload(cfg)` for changed configs
- `setOffsetSeed(labels)` - hashes hostname + external labels with FNV-64a to spread scrape times across HA pairs

---

### 2. Scrape Pool and Loop (`scrape.go`) - The Workhorse

**Type: `scrapePool`** (not shown in full but referenced heavily)
- Holds a map of `activeTargets` (hash -> `*Target`) and `loops` (hash -> `loop`)
- `Sync(groups)` calls `TargetsFromGroup`, partitions into active vs dropped, then calls `sync(all)`
- `sync(targets)` deduplicates targets by hash, creates new `scrapeLoop` + `targetScraper` for new targets, stops loops for disappeared targets, starts new loops via `go l.run(nil)`

**Type: `scrapeLoop`** - one per target
```go
type scrapeLoop struct {
    ctx, parentCtx, appenderCtx context.Context
    interval, timeout  time.Duration
    scraper            scraper        // HTTP fetcher
    cache              *scrapeCache   // per-target label/metadata cache
    appendable         storage.Appendable
    appendableV2       storage.AppendableV2
    sampleMutator      labelsMutator  // applies metric relabeling
    // config options:
    sampleLimit, bucketLimit int
    honorLabels, honorTimestamps bool
    enableNativeHistogramScraping bool
    convertClassicHistToNHCB bool
    mrc []*relabel.Config
    // ...
}
```

**Scrape loop lifecycle:**
1. `run(errc)` - waits for initial jitter offset (to spread load), then ticks at `interval`; on each tick calls `scrapeAndReport()`; on shutdown writes end-of-run staleness markers (waits 2+ intervals before marking stale)
2. `scrapeAndReport(last, appendTime, errc)`:
   - Creates an appender via `sl.appender()` (wraps `AppendableV2` or `Appendable`)
   - Opens HTTP GET via `sl.scraper.scrape(ctx)` (with timeout)
   - Reads response body into a pooled buffer via `sl.scraper.readResponse()`
   - Calls `app.append(b, contentType, appendTime)` - the core parse-and-store step
   - On parse/limit errors: rolls back, calls `append([]byte{}, ...)` to trigger stale markers
   - On success: commits the appender batch

**Type: `targetScraper`** - the HTTP client
- `scrape(ctx)` - issues an HTTP GET with `Accept`, `Accept-Encoding`, `User-Agent`, and `X-Prometheus-Scrape-Timeout-Seconds` headers; supports OTel tracing
- `readResponse(ctx, resp, w)` - validates HTTP 200, handles gzip decompression (with reader reuse), enforces body size limit

**Type: `scrapeCache`** - per-target, lives for the pool lifetime
```go
type scrapeCache struct {
    iter          uint64
    series        map[string]*cacheEntry    // metric string -> (SeriesRef, lset, hash)
    droppedSeries map[string]*uint64        // dropped metric string -> last iter
    seriesCur     map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // current scrape's seen series
    seriesPrev    map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // previous scrape's seen series
    metadata      map[string]*metaEntry     // metric family name -> (Type, Help, Unit)
}
```
Key methods:
- `get(met []byte)` - cache hit check, marks `lastIter = current iter`
- `addRef(met, ref, lset, hash)` - populates cache on first appearance
- `trackStaleness(ref, ce)` / `forEachStale(f)` - swap `seriesPrev`/`seriesCur` each iteration; series in `seriesPrev` but not `seriesCur` are stale
- `iterDone(flushCache bool)` - evicts stale entries; uses heuristic: flush if cache grew to more than `2*successfulCount + 1000`
- `setType/setHelp/setUnit` - update `metaEntry` in `metadata` map, track `lastIterChange` for WAL write decisions

**Label mutation via `mutateSampleLabels`:**
- `honor_labels=true`: target labels are added only if not already present in the sample
- `honor_labels=false`: conflicting sample labels are renamed to `exported_<name>`, then target labels win
- Relabeling (`MetricRelabelConfigs`) is applied after label merging

---

### 3. Text Format Parsing (`textparse_interface.go`) - The Parser Interface

**Package:** `textparse`

**Interface: `Parser`**
```go
type Parser interface {
    Next() (Entry, error)     // advance; returns EntryInvalid/EOF at end
    Series() ([]byte, *int64, float64)         // metric bytes, optional timestamp, float value
    Histogram() ([]byte, *int64, *histogram.Histogram, *histogram.FloatHistogram)
    Help() ([]byte, []byte)   // metric name, help text
    Type() ([]byte, model.MetricType)
    Unit() ([]byte, []byte)
    Labels(l *labels.Labels)  // writes labels into pre-allocated struct
    Exemplar(l *exemplar.Exemplar) bool  // callable multiple times per series
    StartTimestamp() int64    // created timestamp in ms, 0 if unknown
    Comment() []byte
}
```

**Entry types** (iterator protocol):
```go
const (
    EntryInvalid   = -1
    EntryType      = 0   // TYPE comment line
    EntryHelp      = 1   // HELP comment line
    EntrySeries    = 2   // float64 sample
    EntryComment   = 3   // arbitrary comment
    EntryUnit      = 4   // UNIT comment line
    EntryHistogram = 5   // native histogram sample
)
```

**`New(b []byte, contentType string, st *labels.SymbolTable, opts ParserOptions)`** dispatches to:
- `NewOpenMetricsParser` for `application/openmetrics-text`
- `NewProtobufParser` for `application/vnd.google.protobuf`
- `NewPromParser` for `text/plain`
- Optionally wraps the base parser with `NewNHCBParser` if `ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB=true`

**`ParserOptions`** controls:
- `EnableTypeAndUnitLabels` - attach `__type__`/`__unit__` labels to samples
- `IgnoreNativeHistograms` - suppress native histogram buckets
- `ConvertClassicHistogramsToNHCB` - reformat classic histograms as native custom-bucket histograms
- `OpenMetricsSkipSTSeries` - skip `_created` timestamp series in OpenMetrics format

---

### 4. Counter Internal Representation (`prom_counter.go`)

**Package:** `prometheus` (client_golang)

**Interface: `Counter`** embeds `Metric` and `Collector`; adds `Inc()` and `Add(float64)`

**Struct: `counter`** - split-storage design for performance:
```go
type counter struct {
    valBits uint64        // float64 bits for non-integer adds (atomic CAS loop)
    valInt  uint64        // integer accumulator for Inc() and integer Add() (atomic Add)
    selfCollector
    desc       *Desc
    createdTs  *timestamppb.Timestamp
    labelPairs []*dto.LabelPair
    exemplar   atomic.Value   // *dto.Exemplar, set by AddWithExemplar
    now        func() time.Time
}
```

**Why two fields?** `Inc()` and integer `Add(n)` use `atomic.AddUint64(&c.valInt, n)` - a single cheap atomic. Non-integer `Add(v)` uses a CAS loop on `valBits` to atomically update the float bits. `get()` sums both: `math.Float64frombits(valBits) + float64(valInt)`. This avoids a mutex for the extremely hot `Inc()` path.

**`Write(out *dto.Metric)`** reads exemplar first (to avoid a race where exemplar exists for a value not yet visible), then calls `populateMetric(CounterValue, val, labelPairs, exemplar, out, createdTs)`.

**`CounterVec`** wraps `MetricVec` - a hash-map of label-values -> `Counter` instances. `WithLabelValues("200", "GET")` returns (or creates) the specific counter for those label values.

---

### 5. Gauge Internal Representation (`prom_gauge.go`)

**Interface: `Gauge`** embeds `Metric` and `Collector`; adds `Set`, `Inc`, `Dec`, `Add`, `Sub`, `SetToCurrentTime`

**Struct: `gauge`** - simpler than counter because it can go both directions:
```go
type gauge struct {
    valBits uint64   // atomic store of float64 bits
    selfCollector
    desc       *Desc
    labelPairs []*dto.LabelPair
}
```

**Operations:**
- `Set(val)` - `atomic.StoreUint64(&g.valBits, math.Float64bits(val))` - cheapest, optimized path
- `Add(val)` - CAS loop: load old bits, compute new float, swap; negative `val` means decrease
- `Inc()` = `Add(1)`, `Dec()` = `Add(-1)`, `Sub(val)` = `Add(-val)`
- `SetToCurrentTime()` - sets to `float64(time.Now().UnixNano()) / 1e9`
- `Write(out)` - reads valBits atomically, calls `populateMetric(GaugeValue, val, ...)`

Note: Unlike counters, gauges use a single float CAS path for everything (no integer fast path), which is acceptable because `Set` (the dominant operation for gauges) is a simple atomic store.

---

### 6. Registry - How Metric Families are Managed (`prom_registry.go`)

**Package:** `prometheus` (client_golang)

**Key interfaces:**
- `Registerer` - `Register(Collector)`, `MustRegister(...Collector)`, `Unregister(Collector)`
- `Gatherer` - `Gather() ([]*dto.MetricFamily, error)`

**Struct: `Registry`**
```go
type Registry struct {
    mtx                 sync.RWMutex
    collectorsByID      map[uint64]Collector   // collectorID (XOR of desc IDs) -> Collector
    descIDs             map[uint64]struct{}    // all known desc IDs globally
    dimHashesByName     map[string]uint64      // fqName -> dimHash (label names + help hash)
    uncheckedCollectors []Collector            // collectors with no Describe output
    pedanticChecksEnabled bool
}
```

**`Register(c Collector)`** steps:
1. Calls `c.Describe(descChan)` in a goroutine
2. For each `*Desc`: validates it, XORs its ID into `collectorID`, checks `dimHashesByName` for label name/help consistency
3. If `collectorID` is new: writes `collectorsByID[collectorID] = c`, updates `descIDs` and `dimHashesByName`
4. If a Collector yields no descs: appended to `uncheckedCollectors` (always succeeds, no dedup check)

**`Gather()` algorithm:**
1. Takes RLock to snapshot collectors into channels, releases lock
2. Spawns `collectWorker()` goroutines (one per collector budget) that drain both `checkedCollectors` and `uncheckedCollectors` channels, calling `collector.Collect(metricChan)`
3. Main goroutine drains `checkedMetricChan` and `uncheckedMetricChan`, calling `processMetric()` for each
4. `processMetric()`: writes metric to `dto.Metric`, looks up or creates the `dto.MetricFamily` in `metricFamiliesByName`, validates type consistency, hashes (name + label pairs) to detect duplicates

1. **Buffer pooling** - `pool.New(1e3, 100e6, 3, ...)` reuses byte slices across scrapes, sized to the last scrape's response to avoid repeated allocation.

2. **SeriesRef caching** - `scrapeCache.series` maps the raw metric string bytes to a `cacheEntry{ref, lset, hash}`. On subsequent scrapes of the same series, the `ref` is passed directly to `Append()`, skipping label lookup in the TSDB.

3. **Staleness detection** - two maps `seriesCur` / `seriesPrev` are swapped each iteration. Series absent from `seriesCur` after parsing are stale and get a `StaleNaN` sample appended.

4. **Split counter storage** - the `counter` struct uses two atomics (`valInt` for integer increments, `valBits` for float64 increments) so `Inc()` is a single `atomic.AddUint64` with no CAS retry loop.

5. **Registry ID scheme** - a Collector's identity is the XOR of all its descriptor IDs. This allows O(1) lookup and dedup detection without storing the full descriptor set.

6. **Metric family consistency** - `dimHashesByName` maps a metric's fully-qualified name to a hash of its label names and help string, enforcing that all collectors contributing to the same metric family agree on its schema for the lifetime of the process.

### 1. Scrape Manager (`manager.go`) - The Top-Level Coordinator

**Package:** `scrape`

**Type: `Manager`**
```go
type Manager struct {
    opts         *Options
    appendable   storage.Appendable    // v1 storage interface
    appendableV2 storage.AppendableV2  // v2 storage interface (preferred)
    scrapeConfigs map[string]*config.ScrapeConfig
    scrapePools   map[string]*scrapePool
    targetSets    map[string][]*targetgroup.Group
    graceShut     chan struct{}
    triggerReload chan struct{}
    metrics       *scrapeMetrics
    buffers       *pool.Pool
    mtxScrape     sync.Mutex
}
```

**Key functions:**
- `NewManager(...)` - validates that exactly one of `appendable`/`appendableV2` is supplied, creates `scrapeMetrics`, allocates a byte-pool (`pool.New(1e3, 100e6, 3, ...)`) for reusing read buffers
- `Run(tsets <-chan map[string][]*targetgroup.Group)` - main loop: receives target groups from discovery, debounces into `triggerReload` channel, delegates to `reloader()` goroutine
- `reloader()` - throttled via ticker (default 5s), drains `triggerReload` then calls `reload()`
- `reload()` - for each job that has no pool yet, creates a new `scrapePool`; calls `sp.Sync(groups)` in parallel goroutines
- `ApplyConfig(cfg)` - reloads each pool concurrently (bounded by `GOMAXPROCS`), stops removed pools, calls `sp.reload(cfg)` for changed configs
- `setOffsetSeed(labels)` - hashes hostname + external labels with FNV-64a to spread scrape times across HA pairs

---

### 2. Scrape Pool and Loop (`scrape.go`) - The Workhorse

**Type: `scrapePool`** (not shown in full but referenced heavily)
- Holds a map of `activeTargets` (hash -> `*Target`) and `loops` (hash -> `loop`)
- `Sync(groups)` calls `TargetsFromGroup`, partitions into active vs dropped, then calls `sync(all)`
- `sync(targets)` deduplicates targets by hash, creates new `scrapeLoop` + `targetScraper` for new targets, stops loops for disappeared targets, starts new loops via `go l.run(nil)`

**Type: `scrapeLoop`** - one per target
```go
type scrapeLoop struct {
    ctx, parentCtx, appenderCtx context.Context
    interval, timeout  time.Duration
    scraper            scraper        // HTTP fetcher
    cache              *scrapeCache   // per-target label/metadata cache
    appendable         storage.Appendable
    appendableV2       storage.AppendableV2
    sampleMutator      labelsMutator  // applies metric relabeling
    // config options:
    sampleLimit, bucketLimit int
    honorLabels, honorTimestamps bool
    enableNativeHistogramScraping bool
    convertClassicHistToNHCB bool
    mrc []*relabel.Config
    // ...
}
```

**Scrape loop lifecycle:**
1. `run(errc)` - waits for initial jitter offset (to spread load), then ticks at `interval`; on each tick calls `scrapeAndReport()`; on shutdown writes end-of-run staleness markers (waits 2+ intervals before marking stale)
2. `scrapeAndReport(last, appendTime, errc)`:
   - Creates an appender via `sl.appender()` (wraps `AppendableV2` or `Appendable`)
   - Opens HTTP GET via `sl.scraper.scrape(ctx)` (with timeout)
   - Reads response body into a pooled buffer via `sl.scraper.readResponse()`
   - Calls `app.append(b, contentType, appendTime)` - the core parse-and-store step
   - On parse/limit errors: rolls back, calls `append([]byte{}, ...)` to trigger stale markers
   - On success: commits the appender batch

**Type: `targetScraper`** - the HTTP client
- `scrape(ctx)` - issues an HTTP GET with `Accept`, `Accept-Encoding`, `User-Agent`, and `X-Prometheus-Scrape-Timeout-Seconds` headers; supports OTel tracing
- `readResponse(ctx, resp, w)` - validates HTTP 200, handles gzip decompression (with reader reuse), enforces body size limit

**Type: `scrapeCache`** - per-target, lives for the pool lifetime
```go
type scrapeCache struct {
    iter          uint64
    series        map[string]*cacheEntry    // metric string -> (SeriesRef, lset, hash)
    droppedSeries map[string]*uint64        // dropped metric string -> last iter
    seriesCur     map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // current scrape's seen series
    seriesPrev    map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // previous scrape's seen series
    metadata      map[string]*metaEntry     // metric family name -> (Type, Help, Unit)
}
```
Key methods:
- `get(met []byte)` - cache hit check, marks `lastIter = current iter`
- `addRef(met, ref, lset, hash)` - populates cache on first appearance
- `trackStaleness(ref, ce)` / `forEachStale(f)` - swap `seriesPrev`/`seriesCur` each iteration; series in `seriesPrev` but not `seriesCur` are stale
- `iterDone(flushCache bool)` - evicts stale entries; uses heuristic: flush if cache grew to more than `2*successfulCount + 1000`
- `setType/setHelp/setUnit` - update `metaEntry` in `metadata` map, track `lastIterChange` for WAL write decisions

**Label mutation via `mutateSampleLabels`:**
- `honor_labels=true`: target labels are added only if not already present in the sample
- `honor_labels=false`: conflicting sample labels are renamed to `exported_<name>`, then target labels win
- Relabeling (`MetricRelabelConfigs`) is applied after label merging

**Package:** `textparse`

**Interface: `Parser`**
```go
type Parser interface {
    Next() (Entry, error)     // advance; returns EntryInvalid/EOF at end
    Series() ([]byte, *int64, float64)         // metric bytes, optional timestamp, float value
    Histogram() ([]byte, *int64, *histogram.Histogram, *histogram.FloatHistogram)
    Help() ([]byte, []byte)   // metric name, help text
    Type() ([]byte, model.MetricType)
    Unit() ([]byte, []byte)
    Labels(l *labels.Labels)  // writes labels into pre-allocated struct
    Exemplar(l *exemplar.Exemplar) bool  // callable multiple times per series
    StartTimestamp() int64    // created timestamp in ms, 0 if unknown
    Comment() []byte
}
```

**Entry types** (iterator protocol):
```go
const (
    EntryInvalid   = -1
    EntryType      = 0   // TYPE comment line
    EntryHelp      = 1   // HELP comment line
    EntrySeries    = 2   // float64 sample
    EntryComment   = 3   // arbitrary comment
    EntryUnit      = 4   // UNIT comment line
    EntryHistogram = 5   // native histogram sample

```

**`New(b []byte, contentType string, st *labels.SymbolTable, opts ParserOptions)`** dispatches to:

- `NewProtobufParser` for `application/vnd.google.protobuf`

### 4. Counter Internal Representation (`prom_counter.go`)

**Package:** `prometheus` (client_golang)

**Interface: `Counter`** embeds `Metric` and `Collector`; adds `Inc()` and `Add(float64)`

**Struct: `counter`** - split-storage design for performance:
```go
type counter struct {
    valBits uint64        // float64 bits for non-integer adds (atomic CAS loop)
    valInt  uint64        // integer accumulator for Inc() and integer Add() (atomic Add)
    selfCollector
    desc       *Desc
    createdTs  *timestamppb.Timestamp
    labelPairs []*dto.LabelPair
    exemplar   atomic.Value   // *dto.Exemplar, set by AddWithExemplar
    now        func() time.Time
}
```

**Why two fields?** `Inc()` and integer `Add(n)` use `atomic.AddUint64(&c.valInt, n)` - a single cheap atomic. Non-integer `Add(v)` uses a CAS loop on `valBits` to atomically update the float bits. `get()` sums both: `math.Float64frombits(valBits) + float64(valInt)`. This avoids a mutex for the extremely hot `Inc()` path.

**`Write(out *dto.Metric)`** reads exemplar first (to avoid a race where exemplar exists for a value not yet visible), then calls `populateMetric(CounterValue, val, labelPairs, exemplar, out, createdTs)`.

### 5. Gauge Internal Representation (`prom_gauge.go`)

**Interface: `Gauge`** embeds `Metric` and `Collector`; adds `Set`, `Inc`, `Dec`, `Add`, `Sub`, `SetToCurrentTime`

**Struct: `gauge`** - simpler than counter because it can go both directions:
```go
type gauge struct {
    valBits uint64   // atomic store of float64 bits
    selfCollector
    desc       *Desc
    labelPairs []*dto.LabelPair
}
```

**Operations:**
- `Set(val)` - `atomic.StoreUint64(&g.valBits, math.Float64bits(val))` - cheapest, optimized path
- `Add(val)` - CAS loop: load old bits, compute new float, swap; negative `val` means decrease
- `Inc()` = `Add(1)`, `Dec()` = `Add(-1)`, `Sub(val)` = `Add(-val)`
- `SetToCurrentTime()` - sets to `float64(time.Now().UnixNano()) / 1e9`
- `Write(out)` - reads valBits atomically, calls `populateMetric(GaugeValue, val, ...)`

Note: Unlike counters, gauges use a single float CAS path for everything (no integer fast path), which is acceptable because `Set` (the dominant operation for gauges) is a simple atomic store.

```
                    |       EntryUnit   -> scrapeCache.setUnit()
                    |       EntrySeries ->
                    |           parser.Labels(&lset)
                    |           mutateSampleLabels() -- honor_labels + metric relabeling
                    |           verifyLabelLimits()
                    |           scrapeCache.get(met) -- lookup cached SeriesRef
                    |           if cache miss:
                    |               appender.Append(0, lset, t, v)  -> new SeriesRef
                    |               scrapeCache.addRef(met, ref, lset, hash)
                    |           else:
                    |               appender.Append(ref, lset, t, v)  -- fast path
                    |           scrapeCache.trackStaleness(ref, ce)
                    |           parser.Exemplar(&ex) -> appender.AppendExemplar()
                    |       EntryHistogram ->
                    |           similar, but calls appender.AppendHistogram()
                    |
                    +-- scrapeCache.forEachStale()
                    |       -- series in seriesPrev but not seriesCur
                    |       -- appender.Append(ref, lset, t, StaleNaN)
                    |
                    +-- scrapeCache.iterDone(true)
                    |       -- swap seriesCur/seriesPrev
                    |       -- evict stale cache entries
                    |
                    +-- appender.Commit()
                            -- batch write to WAL + Head memSeries
```

### Key Architectural Decisions

1. **Buffer pooling** - `pool.New(1e3, 100e6, 3, ...)` reuses byte slices across scrapes, sized to the last scrape's response to avoid repeated allocation.

2. **SeriesRef caching** - `scrapeCache.series` maps the raw metric string bytes to a `cacheEntry{ref, lset, hash}`. On subsequent scrapes of the same series, the `ref` is passed directly to `Append()`, skipping label lookup in the TSDB.

3. **Staleness detection** - two maps `seriesCur` / `seriesPrev` are swapped each iteration. Series absent from `seriesCur` after parsing are stale and get a `StaleNaN` sample appended.

4. **Split counter storage** - the `counter` struct uses two atomics (`valInt` for integer increments, `valBits` for float64 increments) so `Inc()` is a single `atomic.AddUint64` with no CAS retry loop.

5. **Registry ID scheme** - a Collector's identity is the XOR of all its descriptor IDs. This allows O(1) lookup and dedup detection without storing the full descriptor set.

6. **Metric family consistency** - `dimHashesByName` maps a metric's fully-qualified name to a hash of its label names and help string, enforcing that all collectors contributing to the same metric family agree on its schema for the lifetime of the process.

## Prometheus Metrics Scraping & Collection — Data Flow

The fixture project at `testdata/fixtures/go` contains excerpts from core Prometheus packages. Here's how the pieces fit together:

---

### 1. Scrape Manager (`manager.go`) — Top-level Coordinator

The `Manager` struct owns a map of **scrape pools**, one per job name:

```go
type Manager struct {
    scrapeConfigs map[string]*config.ScrapeConfig
    scrapePools   map[string]*scrapePool
    targetSets    map[string][]*targetgroup.Group
    appendable    storage.Appendable
    // ...
}
```

**Flow:** `Run()` receives target groups from service discovery on a channel, debounces them via `triggerReload`, and a `reloader()` goroutine (throttled to every ~5s) calls `reload()`. That method creates a `scrapePool` for each new job and calls `sp.Sync(groups)` to reconcile targets.

`ApplyConfig()` handles config reloads — it calls `sp.reload(cfg)` for changed pools and stops removed ones, bounded by `GOMAXPROCS` concurrent goroutines.

---

### 2. Scrape Loop (`scrape.go`) — Per-Target Worker

Each discovered target gets a `scrapeLoop` + `targetScraper` pair:

```go
type scrapeLoop struct {
    scraper     scraper          // HTTP client (targetScraper)
    cache       *scrapeCache     // label/ref cache for this target
    interval    time.Duration
    appendable  storage.Appendable
    sampleMutator labelsMutator  // honor_labels + metric relabeling
    // ...
}
```

**`run()`** ticks at the configured interval (with an initial jitter offset computed via FNV-64a hash to spread HA pairs). Each tick calls **`scrapeAndReport()`**:

1. `targetScraper.scrape(ctx)` — HTTP GET with `Accept`, gzip, timeout headers
2. `targetScraper.readResponse()` — validates 200, decompresses gzip, enforces body size limit
3. `app.append(body, contentType, timestamp)` — the core parse-and-store step (see below)
4. On success: `appender.Commit()`; on error: `Rollback()` + emit stale markers

**Staleness tracking** uses two maps swapped each iteration:

```go
type scrapeCache struct {
    series     map[string]*cacheEntry        // metric bytes → (SeriesRef, labels, hash)
    seriesCur  map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // seen this scrape
    seriesPrev map[storage.SeriesRef]*cacheEntry  // seen last scrape
    metadata   map[string]*metaEntry         // family name → (Type, Help, Unit)
}
```

After parsing, anything in `seriesPrev` but not `seriesCur` gets a `StaleNaN` (`0x7ff0000000000002`) sample appended. Then the maps are swapped for the next iteration.

**SeriesRef caching** is the key optimization: on the first scrape, `Append(0, lset, t, v)` returns a `SeriesRef`. On subsequent scrapes, the cached ref is passed directly, skipping label lookup in TSDB.

---

### 3. Text Format Parsing (`textparse_interface.go`) — Iterator Protocol

```go
type Parser interface {
    Next() (Entry, error)        // EntryType, EntryHelp, EntrySeries, EntryHistogram, ...
    Series() ([]byte, *int64, float64)
    Histogram() ([]byte, *int64, *histogram.Histogram, *histogram.FloatHistogram)
    Help() ([]byte, []byte)
    Type() ([]byte, model.MetricType)
    Labels(l *labels.Labels)
}
```

`New(body, contentType, ...)` dispatches to `PromParser` (text/plain), `OpenMetricsParser`, or `ProtobufParser`. The parse loop in `append()` calls `parser.Next()` repeatedly:

- **`EntryType`/`EntryHelp`/`EntryUnit`** → update `scrapeCache.metadata`
- **`EntrySeries`** → extract labels, apply `mutateSampleLabels()` (honor_labels + relabeling), then `appender.Append(ref, lset, t, v)`
- **`EntryHistogram`** → similar path via `appender.AppendHistogram()`

---

### 4. Counter (`prom_counter.go`) — Split Atomic Design

```go
type counter struct {
    valBits uint64   // float64 bits, updated via CAS loop for non-integer Add()
    valInt  uint64   // integer accumulator for Inc() and integer Add()
    desc       *Desc
    exemplar   atomic.Value
    labelPairs []*dto.LabelPair
}
```

**Why two fields?** `Inc()` uses a single `atomic.AddUint64(&c.valInt, 1)` — no CAS retry. Non-integer `Add(v)` uses a CAS loop on `valBits`. `get()` returns `Float64frombits(valBits) + float64(valInt)`. This keeps the extremely hot `Inc()` path lock-free with one instruction.

`CounterVec` wraps a `MetricVec` — a hash-map from label values (e.g., `"200", "GET"`) to individual `counter` instances.

---

### 5. Gauge (`prom_gauge.go`) — Simple Atomic Float

```go
type gauge struct {
    valBits uint64   // atomic store of float64 bits
    desc       *Desc
    labelPairs []*dto.LabelPair
}
```

- `Set(v)` → `atomic.StoreUint64` (cheapest path, no CAS)
- `Add(v)` → CAS loop (load, compute, swap)
- `Inc()`/`Dec()` → delegate to `Add(±1)`

Simpler than counter because gauges can go both directions — no need for a separate integer fast path.

---

### 6. Registry (`prom_registry.go`) — Metric Family Management

```go
type Registry struct {
    collectorsByID  map[uint64]Collector   // XOR of desc IDs → Collector
    descIDs         map[uint64]struct{}    // all known desc IDs
    dimHashesByName map[string]uint64      // fqName → hash(label names + help)
    uncheckedCollectors []Collector        // collectors with no Describe output
}
```

**`Register(c)`**: calls `c.Describe(ch)`, XORs all descriptor IDs into a `collectorID`, checks for collisions in `descIDs` and schema consistency in `dimHashesByName` (same metric name must always have the same label names + help string).

**`Gather()`**: spawns worker goroutines that call `c.Collect(ch)` for each registered collector. Each emitted `dto.Metric` is grouped into `dto.MetricFamily` by name, checked for type consistency and duplicate label sets (via xxhash), then returned sorted lexicographically.

---

### End-to-End Data Flow

```
Service Discovery
    │  target groups
    ▼
Manager.Run() → reload() → scrapePool.Sync()
    │                           │
    │                    creates scrapeLoop + targetScraper per target
    │                           │
    ▼                           ▼
scrapeLoop.run()  ──ticker──▶  scrapeAndReport()
                                │
                    ┌───────────┼───────────────┐
                    ▼           ▼               ▼
              HTTP GET    textparse.New()   scrapeCache
              (gzip,      Parser.Next()    get()/addRef()
               timeout)   loop:            trackStaleness()
                          Type→metadata
                          Series→Append()
                          Histogram→AppendHistogram()
                                │
                                ▼
                    forEachStale() → Append(StaleNaN)
                                │
                                ▼
                         appender.Commit()
                                │
                                ▼
                    Head (WAL + stripeSeries + MemPostings)
```

The key insight is the **three-layer caching** strategy: `scrapeCache` caches `SeriesRef` values to avoid repeated label resolution in TSDB, pools byte buffers across scrapes to reduce GC pressure, and swaps two maps each iteration for O(1) staleness detection.
